content.decision_question: Should policy be designed and justified on the assumption that a single structural reform can deliver durable equity, or should it explicitly plan for and institutionalize ongoing monitoring and adjustment across decades?
content.evidence_boundary: Longitudinal comparative evidence (e.g., continued tracking of Finnish PISA trends and informal sorting) can show whether erosion of equity gains is common and under what conditions it is mitigated. It cannot establish how much political and institutional commitment a given society is willing or obligated to sustain over multiple decades — that is a question about collective priorities, not a resolvable empirical fact alone.
content.tension: Even the best-supported comparator case (Finland) shows structural delay of tracking produced real but partial and non-permanent equity gains, with later PISA decline and informal 'emphasized classes' sorting re-emerging; this raises the question of whether any single-lever reform (structural or otherwise) can achieve durable equity without sustained multi-decade systemic commitment that may exceed what any single policy proposal can guarantee.
content.title: Durability of equity gains versus the limits of structural design alone
value_poles: recognition of need for sustained multi-generational commitment
